sequences.title: A traditional Seder roadmap
sequences.beginnerContext: Before the first blessing, explain that the Seder follows fourteen named stages and that the host will announce each action. Define the wine or grape juice, greens, matzah, afikoman, bitter herb, meal, and closing songs when first mentioned.
sequences.rationale: These two source-order records provide the clearest compact overview of the traditional sequence and the early ritual logic, making them suitable for an orientation page rather than scattering unexplained Hebrew stage names.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Paltiel Birnbaum, Hebrew Publishing Company, and Aharon N. Varady's digitization in the final source page.

sequences.title: From migration to liberation
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce the people in the story and state the arc plainly: Jacob's family went to Egypt, grew into a people, was enslaved and harmed, cried out, and was heard. Pause after oppression before describing liberation so the emotional movement is intelligible.
sequences.rationale: These source-order passages create a usable traditional Exodus narrative across migration, population growth, forced labor, suffering, and divine response, giving a novice more than keywords without requiring the entire rabbinic exposition.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Birnbaum, Hebrew Publishing Company, and the Varady digitization; note cuts from the longer traditional exposition.

sequences.title: Pesach, matzah, maror, and lived memory
sequences.beginnerContext: Point to each object as it is named and explain it before asking anyone to read: the offering recalls deliverance, matzah recalls hurried departure, and maror recalls the bitterness of slavery. Then connect the symbols to the teaching that every generation enters the story personally.
sequences.rationale: These consecutive records form the source's complete central teaching on the three required symbols and the every-generation obligation, a coherent unit that supplies both ritual meaning and personal connection.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Paltiel Birnbaum, Hebrew Publishing Company, and the Varady digitization; preserve the sequence as a single sourced unit.

sequences.title: From redemption to praise
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests that the story now turns into gratitude and song. State whether the group will read responsively, sing, or listen, and make participation optional.
sequences.rationale: The adjacent passages preserve the transition out of the central telling and into praise, preventing a clipped psalm fragment from appearing without its narrative reason.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit the Birnbaum edition and Varady digitization in the final credits.

sequences.title: Washing and symbolic eating
sequences.beginnerContext: State exactly what happens: guests may wash their own hands or pour water for one another over a basin, then everyone eats matzah, bitter herb with haroset, and the matzah-maror sandwich as directed. Define each food and give allergy-safe alternatives.
sequences.rationale: These source-order records contain the washing and symbolic-eating progression, but the project must supply accessible host instructions around the traditional blessings and compressed ritual prose.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit the Birnbaum source and digitization; note that accessible action directions are project-authored bridges.

sequences.title: Orientation and the first washing
sequences.beginnerContext: Use this before the first ritual to say that there is no single right way to host, that the Seder explores the Exodus through questions and shared experience, and that guests may take turns reading. Identify wine or grape juice and explain both self-washing and pouring for one another over a basin.
sequences.rationale: These source-order records combine the source's welcoming participation guidance with the first blessing and its unusually clear explanation of the two handwashings, creating a practical novice threshold while omitting page furniture and license text.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit JewishBoston and the Jewish Women's Archive and preserve the CC BY-SA 3.0 ShareAlike notice.

sequences.title: Spring greens and broken matzah
sequences.beginnerContext: Point out the green vegetable and salt water, then identify the middle matzah before breaking it. Explain which half becomes the afikoman, that children or any guests may search for it, and that the host may offer a small prize or trade when it is returned.
sequences.rationale: The three adjacent records move naturally from spring and tears to the broken middle matzah and its later return, pairing sensory ritual with a clear narrative image of hurried departure.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit JewishBoston and the Jewish Women's Archive; preserve any separately printed attribution.

sequences.title: Questions, memory, and family history
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests that asking is part of the ritual and that this Haggadah tells Exodus through songs, images, and discussion rather than a single continuous script. Invite each person to choose one concrete family or Passover memory and allow anyone to pass.
sequences.rationale: The two source-order passages explain the source's impressionistic method and then invite family and community stories, a coherent participatory setup that avoids the extracted page-furniture records between them.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit the containing Haggadah and retain any named contributors attached to individual questions.

sequences.title: Women's resistance and the Exodus
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce the arc in plain language: the ancestors reached Egypt, a new ruler enslaved their descendants, Shifra and Puah resisted orders to kill newborn boys, Moses later confronted Pharaoh, plagues struck Egypt, and the people left. Acknowledge Egyptian suffering and explain the cup-diminishing action.
sequences.rationale: These source-order records preserve the source's narrative movement from ancestral beginnings through the midwives' resistance to the plague ritual while excluding unusable page-furniture extractions.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit JewishBoston and the Jewish Women's Archive and preserve the source-presented Temple Emunah attribution.

sequences.title: Traditional symbols and an orange
sequences.beginnerContext: Point to and define the traditional symbols before adding the orange. Explain that the orange is a modern symbol of inclusion and name Susannah Heschel and the history accurately without repeating a false origin story.
sequences.rationale: The two source-order records connect the required ritual-symbol teaching to the source's feminist and LGBTQ-inclusive orange history while omitting an intervening page-furniture record.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit JewishBoston and the Jewish Women's Archive and preserve the source's attribution for Susannah Heschel and the orange ritual.

sequences.title: Miriam's courage and water
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce Miriam as Moses's sister and a leader in her own right. If using a cup of water, explain who fills it, when the table notices it, and that guests may sing, read, discuss, or simply listen.
sequences.rationale: The three contiguous source records develop Miriam from story character to communal symbol and offer several possible ritual locations without pretending there is one fixed practice.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit the containing Haggadah and preserve the source's Tamara Cohen, Debbie Friedman, and other printed attributions.

sequences.title: Blessings, bitter herb, afikoman, and grace
sequences.beginnerContext: Supply the omitted practical steps between these source passages: wash personally or for one another, bless and eat matzah, taste bitter herb with haroset, build the sandwich, eat the meal, retrieve and share the afikoman, then begin grace. Define every food and include allergy substitutions.
sequences.rationale: These source-order milestones retain the usable blessings and meaning of the symbolic foods through post-meal gratitude while avoiding extracted records quarantined as page furniture; they require verified host-direction modules to fill the named gaps.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit JewishBoston and the Jewish Women's Archive and preserve all source-presented blessing and adaptation credits.

sequences.title: Cumulative closing songs
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests that these are playful cumulative songs sung near the end. Teach one response before starting, make singing optional, and stop at a natural verse if the table is ready to close.
sequences.rationale: The three source-order records carry Echad Mi Yodea into Chad Gadya as a coherent participatory ending while avoiding the final extracted typo in the angel-of-death verse.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit JewishBoston and the Jewish Women's Archive and retain any song-source credit printed in the edition.

sequences.title: Broken matzah and inner transformation
sequences.beginnerContext: First identify the middle matzah, explain that the larger half becomes the afikoman, and state how it will be hidden and returned. Then invite guests to treat the break as an optional reflection on what is unfinished and what might become whole.
sequences.rationale: These source-order passages connect the physical act of breaking matzah with the source's central inner-Seder method, offering spiritual depth grounded in a visible ritual rather than an abstract meditation.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rabbi David Seidenberg and neohasid.org; preserve CC BY-SA 4.0 attribution, change notice, and ShareAlike terms.

sequences.title: Why this story is retold
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests that Maggid is the storytelling section and that the story belongs to the whole table. Invite people to take turns reading, while allowing anyone to pass.
sequences.rationale: The three adjacent records establish the obligation to tell, expand it into active participation, and frame the narrative as lived rather than merely historical, a coherent orientation to Maggid.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Seidenberg and neohasid.org and indicate any abridgment.

sequences.title: Four ways of approaching the story
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce these as four modes that can exist within any person, not fixed labels for actual children. Explain unfamiliar spiritual vocabulary and do not ask guests to identify one another.
sequences.rationale: Five consecutive records preserve the source's contemplative reinterpretation as a complete psychological movement, avoiding the harm of extracting a single label without its balancing perspectives.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rabbi David Seidenberg and preserve any attribution attached to adapted Four Children material.

sequences.title: A compact Torah story
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce the main people and sequence before reading: ancestors migrate, descendants are enslaved, oppression intensifies, the people cry out, and liberation begins. Clarify that the text is a compact scriptural retelling rather than the whole story.
sequences.rationale: The adjacent records condense the biblical and rabbinic arc into a source-led narrative unit that can support a shorter Haggadah without reducing Exodus to isolated terms.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Seidenberg and neohasid.org under CC BY-SA 4.0; indicate any cuts.

sequences.title: Oppression, cry, and responsibility
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that the traditional verses linger over what oppression does to families and how a cry becomes heard. Invite one concrete question about whose cries society notices today, without requiring personal disclosure or making unsupported current claims.
sequences.rationale: Six adjacent records form a sustained ethical exposition from forced labor and family harm through the people's cry and the obligation to notice vulnerability, one of the source's strongest justice-centered movements.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rabbi David Seidenberg and neohasid.org; retain CC BY-SA 4.0 and any item-level credits.

sequences.title: Symbols becoming lived memory
sequences.beginnerContext: Point to the matzah and maror and define them before moving into meditation. Explain that 'in every generation' asks guests to connect memory to present responsibility, not to pretend they literally lived in ancient Egypt.
sequences.rationale: The seven consecutive source records keep ritual objects, embodied interpretation, and the every-generation teaching in one complete movement, making the contemplative layer legible and grounded.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Seidenberg and neohasid.org and preserve source-presented attributions for any embedded interpretation.

sequences.title: Mindful washing and symbolic eating
sequences.beginnerContext: Give the practical instructions before reflection: guests may wash their own hands or pour for one another over a basin, then eat matzah, bitter herb with haroset, and the sandwich. Keep any silence or visualization optional.
sequences.rationale: The four adjacent passages bring the source's contemplative voice into the actual table sequence while preserving the correct ritual order, so meaning follows an understandable action.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rabbi David Seidenberg and neohasid.org; identify project-authored action bridges separately.

sequences.title: Grace, enoughness, and peace
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that Barech is gratitude after eating and invite guests to notice sustenance, interdependence, and the wish for peace. State whether the table will read all four paragraphs or one selected portion.
sequences.rationale: These source-order passages form an inward-to-outward arc from food and sufficiency to collective wellbeing and peace, giving the contemplative source a coherent closing table movement.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Seidenberg and neohasid.org under CC BY-SA 4.0 and preserve embedded credits.

sequences.title: The tomato as labor and liberation
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that some contemporary Seders add a tomato to acknowledge the farmworkers who bring food to the table; identify CIW as the Coalition of Immokalee Workers before reading.
sequences.rationale: These adjacent passages introduce one symbol, explain both its bitterness and its liberatory meaning, and end with a direct ritual invitation rather than a disconnected policy lesson.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit T'ruah and Rabbi Michael Rothbaum on the final sources page; preserve the Universal Declaration attribution contained in the passage.

sequences.title: Korekh and survivor-led liberation
sequences.beginnerContext: Before making the Hillel sandwich, explain that Korekh combines bitter maror and sweet charoset; offer a content note for trafficking testimony and tell guests they may pass on the reading.
sequences.rationale: The sequence begins with Korekh's mixed taste, lets survivors speak in their own words, and then explicitly rejects savior-centered storytelling in favor of affected communities' leadership.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit T'ruah, the quoted survivors as presented by the source, and Elena Stein on the final sources page; retain the Life Interrupted locator if this sequence is published.

sequences.title: Tzafun: seek what is hidden
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that Tzafun means hidden and is the moment when the afikoman returns; frame the school and restaurant campaign as dated examples of turning learning into action, not as instructions for a current campaign.
sequences.rationale: The passages sustain one arc from the hidden matzah to a guiding North Star and then to concrete, age-appropriate student action, making the source useful for participatory family tables.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit T'ruah, Rabbi Laurie Hahn Tapper, and Rabbi Jesse M. Olitzky on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Birkat Hamazon and a life of dignity
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce Barech as the blessing after the meal and explain that this reading interprets one traditional plea for sustenance and shelter through the lens of coerced labor.
sequences.rationale: The second passage completes the first passage's interpretation and turns a difficult subject into a clear universal claim: every person seeks a life of dignity.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit T'ruah and Rabbi Raysh Weiss on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Four Children: from awareness to agency
sequences.beginnerContext: First explain that the traditional Haggadah imagines four different kinds of questioners; this adaptation uses them as stages in learning about forced labor and finding a useful response.
sequences.rationale: The pair moves coherently from not knowing, through overwhelm, toward joy, agency, and responsibility without suggesting that a guest must personally solve a vast injustice.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit T'ruah and Keeli Sorensen on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Maggid with heart, body, humility, and song
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that Maggid is the long storytelling section and that the Haggadah asks each person to imagine having left Egypt, while this reading also cautions against confusing imagination with another person's lived experience.
sequences.rationale: Four contiguous source records complete the argument about embodied storytelling, empathy, privilege, and responsible participation, then carry the source's transition into song without breaking a contributor quotation or sentence.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit T'ruah, Rabbi David Spinrad, Claudia Cojocaru, and Rabbi Angela W. Buchdahl; preserve every printed attribution and separately review the artwork and song rights.

sequences.title: Karpas and nourishment for a long journey
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests to take a green vegetable, dip it in salt water, and taste it; then explain that the source connects this first dipping to both tears and the Israelites' search for drinkable water after crossing the sea.
sequences.rationale: This is a complete ritual explanation with sensory instructions, an Exodus episode, and a practical insight about sustaining oneself during a long struggle.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit T'ruah on the final sources page and identify the Exodus 15:22-27 reference retained in the reading.

sequences.title: From spring festival to Freedom Seder
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce this as the source author's account of how Passover imagery traveled through Jewish, Christian, Muslim, and Black freedom traditions, and explain that the sequence ends with the first 1969 Freedom Seder.
sequences.rationale: The four passages form one chronological narrative from ancient spring practices through Exodus traditions and the civil-rights era to the first Freedom Seder.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rabbi Arthur Waskow and The Shalom Center; preserve the Ahmed Ali Qur'an translation attribution and the named historical figures.

sequences.title: Miriam's water and creative freedom
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that Miriam's Cup is filled with water in honor of Miriam, whose well sustained the Israelites in the wilderness; identify the passage as an interfaith and diasporic meditation on water.
sequences.rationale: The sequence moves from a cup ritual to African diasporic water wisdom, then to Miriam's actions and a poem, maintaining water as the organizing image throughout.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit The Shalom Center, Michael Twitty, and Muriel Rukeyser on the final sources page, preserving the source's placement of their contributions.

sequences.title: Three tastes of transformation
sequences.beginnerContext: Place the matzah, bitter herb, and charoset where everyone can see them; tell guests when to lift and taste each item and define charoset as the sweet fruit-and-nut mixture on the Seder plate.
sequences.rationale: These passages give a complete sensory progression from matzah to maror to charoset and explain how bitter memory and sweet possibility coexist at the table.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rabbi Arthur Waskow, The Shalom Center, and The Freedom Seder for the Earth on the final sources page.

sequences.title: An unfinished Dayenu and embodied action
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain Dayenu as a cumulative song meaning 'it would have been enough,' then tell guests that this adaptation deliberately answers that justice work is not complete after any single achievement.
sequences.rationale: The sequence carries one argument from an ecological and social-justice Dayenu, through interfaith intention and civil-rights sit-ins, to choosing a concrete action after the Seder.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit The Shalom Center, Ali Shariati, Rabbi Arthur Waskow, and the source-presented song writers or traditions on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Elijah, the afikoman, and responsibility
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain Elijah's Cup, tell children that the afikoman is the hidden matzah they may search for, and state in advance that any donation named in the activity is voluntary and chosen by the adults.
sequences.rationale: The passages make a coherent closing movement from accepting responsibility, through song and the children's afikoman search, to a communal reading about carrying one another toward justice and mercy.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit The Shalom Center, Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., Pete Seeger, and the source-presented author of the closing reading on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Why liberation is told
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce the Haggadah as a guide to a participatory Passover meal and explain that the story moves between ancient slavery and present responsibility. Invite guests to take turns reading and allow anyone to pass.
sequences.rationale: The early source-order records establish the edition's liberation purpose and participatory method, giving later justice readings a clear authorial frame rather than presenting them as isolated inserts.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Emily Aviva Kapor-Mater and the CC BY 4.0 edition; indicate any abridgment.

sequences.title: Questions opening the telling
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that asking questions is a ritual act and that Maggid is the storytelling section. Give the Four Questions a clear reader cue, then say in plain language why the group turns from questions to the Exodus story.
sequences.rationale: These adjacent records retain the question-and-response movement that launches Maggid without importing the preceding modern Israel refrain, so the sequence works in the default peace-focused edition.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Kapor-Mater and preserve any source-presented attribution for translations or songs.

sequences.title: Four children and four questions
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce the figures as four ways people may approach a difficult story, not labels for actual children. Translate unfamiliar terms, make reading roles optional, and give the table one strong follow-up question rather than asking guests to classify themselves.
sequences.rationale: Seven consecutive records preserve the source's full reinterpretive arc and its internal balance; using the whole movement avoids a harsh or confusing child label appearing without response.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Kapor-Mater and retain any separately named contributor associated with the Four Children interpretation.

sequences.title: Oppression, departure, and diminished joy
sequences.beginnerContext: State the story arc before reading: the Israelites were enslaved, Moses confronted Pharaoh, plagues struck Egypt, and the people left. Explain the cup-diminishing action and say that freedom is not celebrated by ignoring Egyptian suffering.
sequences.rationale: Five adjacent passages provide a continuous Exodus-and-plagues movement with ritual response, giving novices enough narrative to understand the action and its moral complexity.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Kapor-Mater under CC BY 4.0 and preserve any credited plague interpretation.

sequences.title: Crossing, Miriam, and gratitude
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that the story reaches the sea, where escape becomes possible and Miriam leads song. Give guests a singing or spoken-response option, then introduce Dayenu as a gratitude chain rather than a celebration of anyone's destruction.
sequences.rationale: The five contiguous records preserve the narrative turn from danger through crossing and women's leadership into gratitude, a cohesive source-led emotional arc.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Kapor-Mater and preserve printed song, translation, and Miriam attributions.

sequences.title: The three symbols and every generation
sequences.beginnerContext: Point to each item and define it before the reading. Explain that the three symbols carry the story into the body and that 'every generation' asks what freedom requires now.
sequences.rationale: Six consecutive records contain the complete symbol-and-memory unit, allowing the source's liberation framing to grow directly out of ritual objects rather than arrive as an unrelated theme paragraph.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Kapor-Mater and retain any source-presented attribution attached to the interpretations.

sequences.title: Matzah, sandwich, meal, and afikoman
sequences.beginnerContext: Move one action at a time: bless and eat matzah, explain the bitter herb and haroset before building the sandwich, eat the meal, then retrieve and share the afikoman. Define every food and include allergy alternatives; supply the missing maror action from a separately verified source module.
sequences.rationale: Six source-order records preserve the usable table sequence from matzah through sandwich, meal, and afikoman while deliberately omitting a run-together OCR record that cannot yet provide exact maror text.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Emily Aviva Kapor-Mater and preserve CC BY 4.0 attribution and change notice.

sequences.title: Gratitude turning toward peace
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that after the meal the table gives thanks and widens the wish for wellbeing to everyone. Identify responsive lines, make prayer language optional for mixed-belief groups, and end with a concrete wish for peace and shared dignity.
sequences.rationale: The six source-order records build a coherent post-meal movement from gratitude through collective care into peace, fitting the project's required framing without an isolated political slogan.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Kapor-Mater and preserve the attribution of any embedded prayer, poem, or translation.

sequences.title: Preparing people, objects, and expectations
sequences.beginnerContext: Use this before the Seder to explain what guests will read, eat, ask, and do. Add a simple checklist naming the plate, matzah, wine or grape juice, salt water, basin, towels, and optional additions.
sequences.rationale: The three adjacent preparation records form an unusually strong host-and-guest orientation in the source's own voice, reducing the need for generic AI-written setup.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rabbi Rachel Barenblat and observe the share-with-credit, do-not-sell condition.

sequences.title: Entering sacred time together
sequences.beginnerContext: Say that the first cup contains wine or grape juice and that the opening marks a change from ordinary dinner to a shared ritual. Explain the first handwashing and offer both self-washing and pouring for one another over a basin.
sequences.rationale: The opening records create a cohesive threshold from gathering and intention into the first ritual acts, well suited to the source's contemplative voice.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Barenblat and preserve any printed credit attached to the opening poem or blessing.

sequences.title: Questions that open the story
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that Maggid is the section where the table tells the Exodus story and that questions are part of the ritual. Assign short readings around the table, let guests pass, and give younger participants concrete prompts.
sequences.rationale: Six consecutive source passages move from ritual questioning into the obligation and permission to retell, preserving the source's invitation to make the Haggadah partly one's own.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Barenblat and retain every source-presented contributor credit in this movement.

sequences.title: Slavery, courage, and the road out
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce the main figures and state the full arc: a ruler enslaved the Israelites, resistance and courage kept hope alive, Moses confronted Pharaoh, plagues brought suffering, and the people escaped. Pause for compassion before celebrating freedom.
sequences.rationale: The eight source-order segments preserve a complete modern retelling with narrative momentum, making it a strong source-led backbone rather than a collection of thematic keywords.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rabbi Rachel Barenblat and all individually credited writers appearing in the selected narrative.

sequences.title: Plate symbols and an inclusive table
sequences.beginnerContext: Point to each named plate object and explain its practical use before its symbolism. If an orange or other optional item appears, say why this table chose it and give an accurate history.
sequences.rationale: The adjacent records join traditional plate explanation to the source's inclusive additions, letting personalization grow from the ritual object rather than from a detached theme card.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Barenblat and preserve any named originator or quoted source attached to each optional plate practice.

sequences.title: From handwashing to the Hillel sandwich
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell everyone exactly what to do, one action at a time: wash personally or pour for one another, eat matzah, taste bitter herb with haroset, then make the sandwich. Define haroset, explain the blessings, and include allergy substitutions.
sequences.rationale: Eight consecutive records preserve the correct practical and emotional movement through the symbolic foods, one of the source's clearest ready-to-lead ritual sequences.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Barenblat and preserve printed credits for blessings, translations, poems, or meditations in the sequence.

sequences.title: Opening the door and filling the cup
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain Elijah's cup, Miriam's cup, and who will open the door or pour water. Present hope and welcome as the purpose of the actions and make song or prayer participation optional.
sequences.rationale: Five adjacent records preserve the source's paired treatment of Elijah and Miriam, balancing inherited hope with women's leadership and the symbolism of sustaining water.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Barenblat and every separately named author or song source appearing in the sequence.

sequences.title: Carrying freedom beyond the table
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests that Nirtzah closes the formal order but sends the work of freedom into the year ahead. Offer one final shared line and one optional reflection rather than introducing a new discussion.
sequences.rationale: The four source-order records create a complete contemplative ending, resolving the source's themes instead of stopping after a song or mid-sentence invitation.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rabbi Rachel Barenblat and observe the noncommercial share-with-credit permission.

sequences.title: First cup in the reconstructed rite
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that this is a reconstructed ancient Land of Israel form of the first-cup blessing, then tell guests whether to read the English, Hebrew, or both.
sequences.rationale: These adjacent passages complete the first-cup blessing and its seasonal additions without jumping to a different ritual moment.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur; identify the CC BY-SA 4.0 edition and any adaptation.

sequences.title: Questions leading into servitude
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce the Four Questions as observations about what makes this night different, and explain that the response begins the story of oppression and the cry for freedom.
sequences.rationale: The source moves directly from the questions into its compact account of forced labor, prayer, and deliverance, preserving the question-and-answer logic of Maggid.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur; retain source attribution on the final credits page.

sequences.title: From ancestral beginnings to the plagues
sequences.beginnerContext: Before reading, give a short spoken bridge: the Haggadah widens the Exodus story by tracing the family’s earlier migrations, enslavement, cry, and divine response.
sequences.rationale: Five consecutive passages carry one sustained biblical exposition from ancestral history through oppression and deliverance to the naming of the ten plagues.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur and preserve CC BY-SA 4.0 attribution and ShareAlike conditions.

sequences.title: Passover, matzah, and maror explained
sequences.beginnerContext: Point to each ritual food as it is named and explain that these passages connect what guests can see and taste to the remembered movement from bondage toward freedom.
sequences.rationale: The three adjacent records give the source’s complete explanation of the central symbols and then turn naturally from explanation toward gratitude and song.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur; indicate any abridgment while retaining the CC BY-SA 4.0 terms.

sequences.title: Praise after the freedom story
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests that the story now shifts into poetry: the table responds to liberation with Psalms and a historical liturgical composition.
sequences.rationale: These source-adjacent passages preserve the movement from Psalm 113 into the sea imagery of Psalm 114 and the rite’s added poetic retelling.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur and retain any passage-level attributions resolved from the canonical source.

sequences.title: Matzah, meal, and table blessing
sequences.beginnerContext: Separate the practical actions from the source reading: first explain what everyone eats and when, then identify the transition from the meal to the third cup and grace.
sequences.rationale: The sequence follows the source’s own order from eating matzah and maror through the meal into the opening movements of blessing after food.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur; adaptations remain subject to CC BY-SA 4.0.

sequences.title: Nishmat and embodied praise
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce this as an extended closing poem in which breath, body, language, and community become instruments of gratitude; it belongs only in a spacious edition.
sequences.rationale: Eight consecutive segments preserve a complete long-form arc of Nishmat rather than extracting isolated devotional sentences from the middle of the prayer.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur; preserve CC BY-SA 4.0 attribution, change notice, and ShareAlike requirements.

sequences.title: A place and a voice at the table
sequences.beginnerContext: Invite each guest to put one personally meaningful object near their place, and explain that people may take turns reading, ask questions, request slower speech, or pass.
sequences.rationale: These passages provide the exact orientation a first-time table needs: what Passover recalls, what Seder means, how symbolic objects work, and how shared reading and questions will happen.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Seder in the Streets and all four compilers on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Food, hunger strike, and the narrow place
sequences.beginnerContext: State that this is historical material from a 2014 foodless solidarity Seder, define a hunger strike, and explain that the Hebrew name Mitzrayim can also evoke a narrow or constricted place.
sequences.rationale: The pair explains why the original event changed a ritual meal into a foodless protest and then supplies the source's deliberate, non-anti-Arab language for naming the biblical oppressor.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Seder in the Streets and its compilers; retain the source's 2014 event context on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Miriam's water, handwashing, and karpas
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that this source used water instead of wine in solidarity with hunger strikers; for a home Seder, identify the first cup separately and tell guests exactly when to wash and when to dip a green vegetable in salt water.
sequences.rationale: The two passages maintain water as a continuous symbol from Miriam's well to ritual handwashing and then explain karpas through spring, tears, and renewal.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Seder in the Streets and the Lerman Family Haggadah as presented by the source.

sequences.title: Yachatz and separated families
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell the host to take the middle of three matzot, break it, return the smaller piece, and wrap and hide the larger afikoman; explain that this adaptation connects the broken pieces with families separated by detention and deportation.
sequences.rationale: The second passage completes the first passage's quotation and then moves naturally into Maggid, preserving one coherent metaphor of separation, return, and freedom.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Seder in the Streets, JFREJ's Immigrant Justice/Racial Justice Haggadah insert, and JCA's Immigrant Rights Freedom Seder on the final sources page.

sequences.title: The Four Questions with answers
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that the Four Questions are traditionally asked by the youngest willing participant but may be shared by anyone; point to the matzah, maror, dipping foods, and reclining as each is named.
sequences.rationale: The first passage supplies all four questions and the next two answer each one in direct, accessible language before opening a separate optional discussion.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Seder in the Streets, the DC Avodah Haggadah, and The JOI Haggadah on the final sources page.

sequences.title: A compact Exodus narrative
sequences.beginnerContext: Before reading, briefly explain that Joseph had earlier helped Egypt survive a famine and that the Israelites' descendants remained there; this supplies the one missing setup before the source begins with the new Pharaoh.
sequences.rationale: The three passages give a continuous beginner-readable story from enslavement and Moses' birth through the burning bush, the demand for freedom, and the plagues, ending with compassion for Egyptian suffering.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Seder in the Streets and its four compilers on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Chanted questions and teaching
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain the ritual actions in English first, then identify the Hebrew portions selected for chanting: the invitation, the Four Questions, the duty to tell, and the Four Children.
sequences.rationale: These consecutive source records preserve the complete opening movement of Maggid instead of extracting a trope-marked line without its ritual and narrative setting.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and identify the CC BY-SA 4.0 tropified edition; indicate any cuts.

sequences.title: Chanted ancestry, oppression, and cry
sequences.beginnerContext: Give guests the Exodus arc in clear English before this selection: the family migrated, grew, was enslaved, cried out, and was heard.
sequences.rationale: The three adjacent Hebrew records form a sustained traditional exposition from ancestral migration through oppression and the response to the enslaved people’s cry.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer; retain CC BY-SA 4.0 attribution and ShareAlike requirements.

sequences.title: Plagues leading into Dayenu
sequences.beginnerContext: Frame the plagues with sorrow for suffering, tell guests how to diminish the cup, and explain that Dayenu shifts from catastrophe toward gratitude.
sequences.rationale: The source’s consecutive progression from redemption imagery through the plague list and rabbinic expansion into Dayenu preserves the traditional narrative turn.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and preserve the CC BY-SA 4.0 license and change notice.

sequences.title: Matzah, maror, and lived memory
sequences.beginnerContext: Point to the matzah and maror, summarize their meanings in English, and explain that the text asks every generation to experience the freedom story personally before moving into praise.
sequences.rationale: These adjacent passages complete the central ritual-symbol explanations, the every-generation teaching, and the source’s direct transition into Hallel.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and identify the tropified CC BY-SA 4.0 source in final credits.

sequences.title: Eating rituals into grace after meals
sequences.beginnerContext: Use the project’s English host directions to say exactly what each person washes, eats, and drinks; present this Hebrew only as an optional liturgical layer.
sequences.rationale: The source records follow one uninterrupted table sequence from the second cup through washing and symbolic eating into the opening of grace after the meal.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer; retain CC BY-SA 4.0 attribution, change notice, and ShareAlike terms.

sequences.title: Closing cumulative songs
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests that these are playful cumulative songs traditionally sung at the end; provide a refrain cue and make participation optional.
sequences.rationale: Four final source-order segments preserve the complete rising pattern of Echad Mi Yodea and the continuation of Chad Gadya as a coherent closing activity.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and preserve the source’s CC BY-SA 4.0 conditions.

sequences.title: Preparing by removing leaven
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that searching for and nullifying hametz is a pre-Passover practice, not something guests must improvise at the table. Give a short practical definition of leaven and clearly mark this as optional preparation.
sequences.rationale: The adjacent opening records preserve a complete preparatory ritual and its meaning, useful for a host guide or traditional appendix rather than an unexplained first page.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Eve Levavi Feinstein and Efraim Feinstein and preserve CC BY-SA 4.0 attribution and ShareAlike terms.

sequences.title: Four Questions into Maggid
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain the salt water and broken matzah actions before the youngest willing person or another guest asks the Four Questions. Define Maggid and state that the questions lead into the Exodus story.
sequences.rationale: The three adjacent segments connect preliminary ritual actions with the formal questions and story opening, retaining the intended order rather than detaching the blessing text.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit the Feinsteins and indicate any cuts to the bilingual text.

sequences.title: The traditional Four Children
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce the figures as inherited teaching types, not judgments about actual children or guests. Explain the historical language, avoid assigning labels around the table, and give a gentler discussion prompt.
sequences.rationale: The two adjacent records preserve the traditional Four Children unit and its responses, stopping at a complete sentence before the source turns to a new scriptural exposition. Keeping the figures together is safer and more intelligible than extracting only the severe description of one child.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Eve Levavi Feinstein and Efraim Feinstein; identify this as a traditional teaching in their translation.

sequences.title: From ancestry to oppression
sequences.beginnerContext: State the narrative arc before the detailed verses: the family went to Egypt, became a people, and was enslaved and harmed. Explain that the people's cry and liberation continue in the following part of Maggid. Introduce each scriptural quotation with one sentence of plain-language meaning.
sequences.rationale: Five source-order segments preserve the traditional verse-by-verse movement from ancestry through oppression and stop after a complete scriptural sentence. The later page records were removed because their extracted text ends mid-exposition.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit the Feinsteins and preserve CC BY-SA 4.0 and any embedded source note.

sequences.title: Plagues with diminished joy
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that the plagues are catastrophes within the liberation story, name the cup-diminishing custom, and say explicitly that the table does not celebrate civilian suffering. Use wine or grape juice and give one action cue before the list.
sequences.rationale: The adjacent passages retain the narrative and ritual treatment of the plagues while stopping before the most elaborate numerical multiplication, making a more usable adult or mixed-age unit.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Eve Levavi Feinstein and Efraim Feinstein and indicate any abridgment of the traditional plague exposition.

sequences.title: Gratitude, symbols, and the turn to praise
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce Dayenu as a cumulative gratitude song, then point to the traditional symbols and explain each one before the every-generation teaching. Signal the transition from telling into praise.
sequences.rationale: Seven consecutive passages preserve the source's complete theological and ritual turn from gratitude through Pesach, matzah, maror, lived memory, and Hallel.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit the Feinsteins, preserve ShareAlike terms, and identify omitted Dayenu verses if abridged.

sequences.title: Washing, matzah, and bitter herb
sequences.beginnerContext: Give practical instructions before the liturgy: wash hands individually or by pouring for one another, eat matzah, then bitter herb with haroset and the sandwich. Define haroset and provide allergy-safe substitutions.
sequences.rationale: The three adjacent source records hold the table rituals in their proper order and can serve as a concise traditional block when paired with accessible host directions.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Eve Levavi Feinstein and Efraim Feinstein; distinguish project-authored action cues from source text.

sequences.title: Opening grace after the meal
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that Barech is the gratitude said after eating and tell guests whether the leader will read or everyone will respond. Offer a shortened path without implying that a mixed-belief guest must affirm every theological line.
sequences.rationale: The four source-order records provide a coherent entrance into grace and communal response, retaining the liturgical progression instead of using a blessing fragment out of context.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit the Feinsteins and preserve the CC BY-SA 4.0 notice and change statement.

sequences.title: Translator's critical historical frame
sequences.beginnerContext: Identify this as the 2025 translator's introduction to a 1919 socialist parody, not part of the original ritual; tell guests that the warning is required before any other excerpt from this source.
sequences.rationale: The three passages form a complete responsible-use frame, naming the work's power while directly warning about racist historical ideas, anti-religious reduction, and revolutionary violence.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Shlomo Jack Enkin Lewis as the 2025 translator and preserve the Foundation for Yiddish Culture support acknowledgment contained in the passage.

sequences.title: Hunger, Four Questions, and self-liberation
sequences.beginnerContext: After the translator's warning, explain that this is a 1919 class-parody of searching for chametz, Ha Lachma Anya, the Four Questions, Avadim Hayinu, and the command to remember the Exodus.
sequences.rationale: Skipping only the intervening Yiddish counterparts, these English passages preserve the source's continuous argument from hunger at home through four class questions to its secular reading of liberation in this life.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit the original authors and Shlomo Jack Enkin Lewis's translation on the final sources page; note that intervening Yiddish counterparts are omitted, not reordered.

sequences.title: Workers who built the world
sequences.beginnerContext: Frame this as a period labor lament written after the First World War; explain the source's contrast between what workers produce and what they are able to keep, and offer a pass option for its harsh imagery.
sequences.rationale: The English passages form a sustained account of starvation wages, housing and food insecurity, war, forced work, and bodily exhaustion, ending on the complete question of whether anyone hears the worker's cry.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit the 1919 authors and Shlomo Jack Enkin Lewis's 2025 English translation on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Maror, freedom, and equality
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that the text substitutes Freedom, Equality, and Socialism into familiar Maror and Hallel formulas; distinguish this historical parody from a blessing or creed endorsed by the app.
sequences.rationale: Across alternating bilingual pages, the English sequence completes one liturgical arc from bodily bitterness to freedom, equality, women's dignity, liberation imagery, and collective self-reliance.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit the original authors and Shlomo Jack Enkin Lewis; identify this as a translated parody of traditional Haggadah and Hallel formulas.

sequences.title: Progress and the public good
sequences.beginnerContext: Describe this as a historical secular hymn that places faith in Progress and democratic control of shared prosperity; remind guests that the translator critiques the source's theory of progress.
sequences.rationale: The English passages continue across the alternating Yiddish pages as one sustained closing hymn, ending with collective governance rather than an incomplete page fragment.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit the original authors and Shlomo Jack Enkin Lewis's translation, noting that the sequence follows the English pages of a bilingual edition.

sequences.title: Wisdom for the journey and four cups
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that the traditional Seder has four cups and an additional cup for Elijah; this source dedicates the cups to four connected social problems and the Elijah cup to building Dr. King's Beloved Community.
sequences.rationale: The sequence moves from three concise statements of purpose to a clear four-cup structure and then gives the host the physical setup needed to lead it.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit The Shalom Center, Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., Rabbi Abraham Joshua Heschel, and the traditional Haggadah as presented by the source.

sequences.title: Candles between the fires
sequences.beginnerContext: Before lighting candles, warn that the reading names lynching, Auschwitz, Hiroshima, gun violence, and climate disaster; invite anyone to step out, pass, or join only for the final candle blessing.
sequences.rationale: The source-order arc transforms destructive fire into candlelight, names difference without hierarchy, and arrives at the first cup and a historically grounded labor-justice reading.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit The Shalom Center and Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.; preserve the Memphis sanitation-workers speech context and date.

sequences.title: Shared matzah and structural compassion
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell each person to break off a piece of matzah and hand it to a neighbor; identify the opening federal-budget sentence as 2018 context before moving to the enduring ritual about sharing bread.
sequences.rationale: The passages turn matzah from affliction into shared freedom and then connect immediate generosity with Dr. King's call to change the structures that produce poverty.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit The Shalom Center, the Jewish Labor Committee, and Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Second cup and ecological power
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce the second cup as this source's environmental cup and identify 'Carbon Pharaohs' as an activist metaphor for fossil-fuel corporations, not a neutral factual label.
sequences.rationale: The sequence begins with dignity in agricultural work, names the source's ecological critique, and completes its interpretive reading of the plagues before the source's dated plague list begins.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rabbi Arthur Waskow and The Shalom Center on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Karpas, charoset, and the earth's rest
sequences.beginnerContext: Place parsley, salt water, and charoset where everyone can reach them; explain that charoset is a sweet fruit-and-nut mixture and that the source connects it to spring, love poetry, land rest, and debt release.
sequences.rationale: These passages supply sensory ritual instructions and sustain one environmental theme from spring greens through charoset to the sabbatical release of land and debt.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit The Shalom Center and Rabbi Shefa Gold, and identify the Leviticus 25 and Deuteronomy 15 passages on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Dayenu and ten ecological healings
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain Dayenu as a step-by-step song of gratitude, then label the listed technologies, investment campaigns, and lobbying strategies as examples proposed in 2018 rather than a current action checklist.
sequences.rationale: The sequence keeps the source's full ten-healings activity together and ends with the cup ritual that interprets environmental action as movement from sweetness through difficulty to deeper sweetness.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rabbi Arthur Waskow, The Shalom Center, and the MLK+50 Freedom Seder on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Elijah and the Beloved Community
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain the custom of opening the door for Elijah and define the Beloved Community as Dr. King's vision of a just society; tell guests they will be invited to name one voluntary action before drinking.
sequences.rationale: The sequence preserves a complete closing arc: open the door, sing against being turned around, imagine a crossing where no one drowns, hear King's call to universal love, share Elijah's cup, and pledge action together.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit The Shalom Center, Rabbi Shawn Israel Zevit, Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., and the source-presented freedom-song tradition on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Miriam's fish
sequences.beginnerContext: After explaining the traditional Passover offering, identify the fish as an optional addition honoring Miriam, water, prophecy, and women's role in the Exodus; provide a non-food or plant-based symbol if needed.
sequences.rationale: The two passages form the source's complete fish unit, moving from Rav Sherira Gaon's three foods through Micah, Psalm 8, and the Talmudic account of women drawing water and fish.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and identify Micah 6:4, Psalm 8:8-10, and Sotah 11b on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Leeks before Dayenu
sequences.beginnerContext: Before Dayenu, explain the Persian Jewish custom and ask consent before any gentle scallion tap; guests may hold or wave a leek instead, and no one should be struck or pressured.
sequences.rationale: The pair contains the full custom, its warning against nostalgia for slavery, a rabbinic parable, and its closing wish for enoughness before the Song of Songs reading.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer, Sifrei Bamidbar 87, and Song of Songs 8:11-14 on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Rav Huna's beets before washing
sequences.beginnerContext: Before the second handwashing, identify beets as an optional Ashkenazi food and explain that the text uses their lasting red color to discuss inherited teaching and covenant.
sequences.rationale: These are the complete beet passages, joining Pesachim, Ezekiel, and a humorous Berakhot teaching while retaining one object and one ritual location.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and identify Pesachim 114b, Ezekiel 16:6, and Berakhot 39a on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Orange segments and belonging
sequences.beginnerContext: Near the end of the meal, explain that the orange is an optional symbol created by Susannah Heschel to affirm lesbians and gay men and, more broadly, people marginalized in Jewish community; distinguish this documented account from the common false legend about a man and a bimah.
sequences.rationale: The three passages preserve Heschel's full explanation, correct the patriarchal retelling she criticized, and close with a scriptural image of a house of prayer open to all peoples.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer, Susannah Heschel, Ritualwell, and Isaiah 56:7-8 on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Maxwell House and Diaspora satire
sequences.beginnerContext: Before Tzafun, identify this as an optional comic reading about Jewish advertising history and the Maxwell House Haggadah; explain the PaRDeS method and make all coffee or after-dinner drinks optional and caffeine-free if desired.
sequences.rationale: The two passages preserve the entire joke from literal advertising history through rabbinic analogy and acrostic, rather than extracting a punchline without its setup.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer, the Maxwell House Haggadah historical reference, and Pesachim 39a on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Turnip matzah in Bergen-Belsen
sequences.beginnerContext: When opening the door for Elijah, offer a Holocaust content note and a pass option, then explain that Pearl Benisch remembered making three symbolic matzot from turnip slices shortly before liberation.
sequences.rationale: The two passages contain the complete survivor account and a hopeful explanation of remembering redemption; stopping here deliberately avoids the following collective-destruction verses.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer, Pearl Benisch, Joan Nathan, and the New York Times article dated April 16, 2008 on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Miriam's Cup after Elijah
sequences.beginnerContext: After Elijah's Cup, fill a separate cup with water and explain that Miriam's Cup honors the prophet Miriam and the sustaining well associated with her in rabbinic tradition.
sequences.rationale: The passages preserve the full unit from the Talmudic source through the spoken declaration and closing bilingual song, with a clear action and placement.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and identify Taanit 9a on the final sources page.

sequences.title: Festival sanctification and Exodus memory
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that this is an extended traditional sanctification passage linking Passover to the Exodus and festival calendar. Offer it only to a table seeking substantial liturgy, define the sea and vengeance imagery, and follow with the simpler wine blessing from a separate verified module.
sequences.rationale: These two adjacent intact records preserve one complete liturgical movement in an otherwise control-character-corrupted Kadesh extraction; keeping them together prevents a partial clause from standing alone.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer, identify the CC BY-SA 4.0 edition, indicate changes, and preserve ShareAlike terms.

sequences.title: Abraham challenges the idols
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce this as a traditional expansion about young Abraham challenging his father's idols and the ruler Nimrod, not part of the basic Exodus outline. Name the characters, summarize the missing transitions, and use it only with an adult or well-facilitated table.
sequences.rationale: The three source-order intact records preserve the central confrontation in the edition's long Judeo-Arabic Abraham narrative while skipping corrupted intervening continuations; a host bridge is essential but the source's distinctive story remains recognizable.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Mayer and separately verify the historical source and translation attribution printed for this Judeo-Arabic narrative.

sequences.title: An Exodus poem from suffering to redemption
sequences.beginnerContext: State the narrative arc before the poem: the Israelites suffered slavery, liberation came through destructive plagues, they crossed the sea, and the liturgy turns toward future celebration. Frame firstborn and enemy-death imagery with sorrow and keep Temple hopes within inherited historical liturgy.
sequences.rationale: Three source-order intact records form a continuous piyyut from oppression through plagues and escape into the redemption blessing, preserving the edition's traditional poetic voice without relying on corrupted core pages.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Mayer and retain the source's attribution for the piyyut and any historical translation.

sequences.title: A Hasidic closing story
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that Nirtzah closes the formal Seder and that this is a historical Hasidic story about longing and readiness. Define the military conscription context, identify the quoted Zion language as inherited in the story, and make the long reading optional.
sequences.rationale: The two adjacent records preserve the complete narrative setup and resolution of the edited Martin Buber story, rather than quoting its final Zion line without its historical frame.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Mayer and preserve the printed Martin Buber and Tales of the Hasidim attribution; confirm item-level reuse status before runtime approval.

sequences.title: Western Yiddish build-well song
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce this as a Western Ashkenazi closing song whose traditional refrain hopes for the Temple's rebuilding. Give a transliteration and one repeated response, explain the historical liturgical meaning, and let guests listen instead of sing.
sequences.rationale: The adjacent intact records preserve the song's historical note, refrain, and cumulative alphabetic continuation as one specialist musical unit.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Mayer and the Western Ashkenazi traditional source identified in the edition; indicate any verses omitted.

sequences.title: Who knows one?
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests this is a cumulative number song near the end of the Seder. Teach the question-and-response pattern, clarify unfamiliar references only when they first appear, and stop at a natural number if time is short.
sequences.rationale: Three consecutive intact records preserve the middle-to-late cumulative structure and the edition's inclusive wording, making a usable participatory closing activity.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Mayer and identify the traditional cumulative song; note any shortened verse range.

sequences.title: A multilingual Chad Gadya guessing game
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that this is an optional language game based on the final Chad Gadya verse, not a required ritual. Read the ordinary English pattern first, divide guests into teams only if they want to play, and avoid assuming knowledge of any language.
sequences.rationale: Four consecutive intact records preserve the game's instructions and first group of Aramaic variants, a coherent specialist activity that should never replace the basic closing orientation.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Mayer and separately preserve or verify the attribution of each translation included in the selected game.

sequences.title: Chad Gadya call and response
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests that Chad Gadya is a playful cumulative end song and teach the repeated line before beginning. Make singing optional, explain that the chain becomes violent in a folktale-like way, and allow an early stop for children or time.
sequences.rationale: Four consecutive intact records preserve the author's melody instruction and the song's rising call-and-response chain, providing a more accessible closing choice than the specialist guessing game.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Mayer and identify Chad Gadya as a traditional song in the final source page.

sequences.title: A medieval English Seder survives
sequences.beginnerContext: Present this as a short historical note before the ritual begins: a 1287 English Jewish manuscript preserves local Seder customs from before the expulsion.
sequences.rationale: The article’s opening three segments establish the manuscript, explain why the rite is distinctive, and introduce its linguistic setting as one coherent documentary frame.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Dávid Kaufmann and Aharon N. Varady; identify the public-domain study and CC BY-SA 4.0 transcription.

sequences.title: Medieval haroset ingredients and authority
sequences.beginnerContext: First explain that haroset is the fruit-and-nut mixture used with the bitter herb, then identify these paragraphs as historical evidence about regional recipes rather than the app’s cooking instructions.
sequences.rationale: The three consecutive passages move from the manuscript’s recipe through comparative regional practice to the English rabbinic authority behind that practice.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Dávid Kaufmann and Aharon N. Varady and retain the transcription’s ShareAlike conditions.

sequences.title: Medieval table order and questions
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain the modern action for each step independently; then use this sequence to show how medieval English Jews ordered the cups, vegetables, matzah, questions, and teaching roles.
sequences.rationale: The records follow the manuscript’s own early-Seder order into the Four Questions and Four Children, making a coherent comparative sequence despite the scholarly commentary around it.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Dávid Kaufmann and Aharon N. Varady; preserve article and transcription attribution.

sequences.title: Distinctive medieval Maggid readings
sequences.beginnerContext: Label this explicitly as a scholar’s comparison of manuscript readings, and summarize the relevant difference before displaying any Hebrew excerpt.
sequences.rationale: These consecutive paragraphs document one cluster of Maggid variants, including omissions, additions, and altered formulations, without detaching a single variant from its analysis.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Dávid Kaufmann and Aharon N. Varady; changes to the transcription must be identified under CC BY-SA 4.0.

sequences.title: Hallel, washing, eating, and afikoman practice
sequences.beginnerContext: Use a modern host cue for every action and identify this source only as evidence of how one medieval community handled Hallel, the second washing, ritual foods, and the afikoman.
sequences.rationale: The sequence stays with the article’s later-Seder procedural discussion and preserves the relationship among washing, blessings, symbolic eating, and the end of the meal.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Dávid Kaufmann and Aharon N. Varady and preserve the transcription license and change notice.

sequences.title: A preserved closing poem
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that the article preserves a medieval poetic addition sung near the end of the Seder, then provide an editorially checked translation or refrain guide.
sequences.rationale: The three final article segments introduce the poem, discuss its textual form, and reproduce its conclusion as one self-contained historical unit.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Dávid Kaufmann and Aharon N. Varady; preserve public-domain and CC BY-SA 4.0 source information.

sequences.title: Preparing for the Seder night
sequences.beginnerContext: Summarize in modern English that the source begins with removing leaven, evening prayers, returning home, and approaching the prepared table.
sequences.rationale: These consecutive pages form the work’s complete transition from pre-Passover preparation into the household Seder rather than isolating a single antiquarian instruction.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Johann Stephan Rittangel and Aharon N. Varady; identify the public-domain original and open transcription.

sequences.title: First cup through the questions
sequences.beginnerContext: Use the app’s ordinary action cues first, then label this as a seventeenth-century Latin account of Kiddush, washing, breaking matzah, invitation, questions, and the first answer.
sequences.rationale: Six source-order segments preserve the continuous early ritual sequence and its handoff into the freedom story.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rittangel and Varady; do not silently correct the historical transcription or OCR.

sequences.title: The duty to tell and the Four Children
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that the text moves from the obligation to retell the Exodus into four different learning postures; use inclusive modern labels outside the exact source excerpt.
sequences.rationale: These adjacent records contain one coherent pedagogical movement from the sages’ all-night telling through the four archetypal children and the instruction to begin for someone who cannot ask.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rittangel and Varady and retain both public-domain and transcription-license information.

sequences.title: Oppression, cry, and redemption
sequences.beginnerContext: Provide a concise English story first: forced labor intensified, the people cried out, their suffering was seen, and the text turns toward deliverance.
sequences.rationale: Seven source-order segments maintain the causal story from oppression through the cry and response, ending with the traditional exposition of signs and wonders while omitting one locally quarantined OCR fragment.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rittangel and Varady; visually verify every OCR-derived line against the cited pages.

sequences.title: Passover, matzah, maror, and lived memory
sequences.beginnerContext: Point to each ritual food and explain its meaning in contemporary English before showing the Latin witness; then explain the move from memory into praise.
sequences.rationale: The source sequence contains the three core symbol explanations, the every-generation teaching, and the transition into Hallel as a single ritual argument.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Rittangel and Varady; retain exact source wording and disclose any separate modern translation.

sequences.title: Hebrew-Latin Dayenu witness
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce Dayenu as a cumulative gratitude song and make clear that this selection is a historical Hebrew-Latin transcription, not the app’s English sing-along version.
sequences.rationale: The four official transcription records preserve the complete Dayenu progression in source order and are more reliable than reconstructing it from scattered OCR pages.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Johann Stephan Rittangel and Aharon N. Varady; identify the official open transcription and its CC BY-SA 4.0 conditions.

sequences.title: Opening a home Seder
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests that this is an 1896 Reform home service with period-specific gender roles and God language. Name the first cup as wine or grape juice, summarize the gathering’s purpose, and invite readers to substitute inclusive titles or pronouns before reading the historical prayer.
sequences.rationale: These consecutive pages create a complete opening arc: gathering at home, gratitude for being together, questions about the night, and the parent blessing, stopping before an incomplete page-column transition.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit J. Leonard Levy and the digitized 1896 public-domain edition; disclose any abridgment.

sequences.title: From migration to Moses's demand
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce this as the opening of one historical retelling, define unfamiliar biblical names as needed, and make clear that this excerpt moves from a migrating family to enslavement and Moses's demand for freedom.
sequences.rationale: Three adjacent segments answer a child’s request for the history and form a continuous arc through Pharaoh's forced labor and Moses's demand, stopping before a scrambled page-column seam.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit J. Leonard Levy; identify the 1896 public-domain home service in the final source list.

sequences.title: The human family and brotherly love
sequences.beginnerContext: Frame this as a period parable about solidarity and shared human dignity, while noting that its gendered phrase ‘Brotherly Love’ reflects its era.
sequences.rationale: The source’s question, mountain parable, resolution, and explicit teaching about one human family form a single sustained universalist argument.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit J. Leonard Levy and the public-domain 1896 edition; preserve the historical wording if quoted exactly.

sequences.title: Scripture readings of water and peace
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that the source assembles biblical lines around rescue, water, learning, and peace; identify the source as a historical compilation rather than a single continuous biblical passage.
sequences.rationale: Four consecutive records preserve the compiler’s designed movement from survival through water imagery to the prophetic hope of learning and weapons transformed into tools.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit J. Leonard Levy and include source-level credit for the public-domain service.

sequences.title: Freedom joined to responsibility
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce this as a long 1896 meditation arguing that liberation carries social responsibility and needs just law; tell guests that dated passages have been omitted.
sequences.rationale: The selected source-order passages sustain the work’s freedom-and-law argument while omitting pages whose racial terminology or broken column boundaries are unacceptable for guest-facing reuse.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit J. Leonard Levy and disclose that the public-domain sequence has been abridged by segment selection.

sequences.title: Spring, dry bones, and renewal
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain that Passover is also a spring festival and that this historical reading moves from seasonal renewal to Ezekiel’s dry-bones vision and questions about life after death.
sequences.rationale: Seven consecutive segments preserve the source’s seasonal and theological development through the dry-bones vision and the source's introduction to immortality, ending at a complete thought.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit J. Leonard Levy and the public-domain 1896 source; retain exact wording if used as a historical reading.

sequences.title: Memory, influence, and closing resolve
sequences.beginnerContext: Name this as a historical closing meditation about how people live on through memory and influence, followed by scripture and a communal resolve.
sequences.rationale: The final eight source segments move coherently from doubt and hope through ethical legacy into scripture and the service’s closing prayer.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit J. Leonard Levy and identify the public-domain Haggadah or Home Service for the Festival of Passover.

sequences.title: Table setup and the Seder order
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain in English that these pages inventory the table and summarize all ritual steps; use the app’s illustrated plate guide for the actual instructions.
sequences.rationale: The two consecutive records form a compact, source-authored overview of preparation and the complete Seder sequence.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Artur Carlos de Barros Basto and the digitization where practical; identify the CC0 1.0 edition.

sequences.title: First cup through the questions
sequences.beginnerContext: Use clear English action cues for the wine or grape juice, parsley, salt water, and matzah, then identify the Portuguese question-and-answer text as an optional historical reading.
sequences.rationale: Four consecutive source passages carry the ritual from Kiddush through karpas and yachatz into the invitation, Four Questions, and opening answer, ending at a complete sentence boundary.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Barros Basto and identify the CC0 1.0 Portuguese edition; disclose any abridgment.

sequences.title: Teaching styles and ancestral memory
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain the Four Children as different ways of engaging, use inclusive modern labels outside the exact source, and then introduce the move backward into ancestral history.
sequences.rationale: The selected pages finish the obligation-to-retell teaching, complete the Four Children dialogue, and flow directly into the source’s account of the ancestors and the promise preceding enslavement.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Barros Basto and the digitization where practical; retain the source title and CC0 1.0 status.

sequences.title: Migration, oppression, cry, and deliverance
sequences.beginnerContext: Before reading, give the story in one English sentence: Jacob’s family migrated to Egypt, grew, was enslaved, cried out, was heard, and moved toward freedom through signs and plagues.
sequences.rationale: Seven consecutive pages provide a complete causal Exodus arc rather than a list of disconnected names or symbols.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Artur Carlos de Barros Basto; identify the CC0 1.0 historical edition.

sequences.title: Dayenu and the three symbols
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain Dayenu as cumulative gratitude, then point to the shank bone or substitute, matzah, and bitter herb as the source explains each symbol.
sequences.rationale: The sequence preserves the end of Dayenu, its summation, and the complete turn into the central Passover, matzah, maror, and every-generation teachings.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Barros Basto and identify the CC0 1.0 source; preserve the source order if excerpted.

sequences.title: Second cup, ritual foods, and grace
sequences.beginnerContext: Give the practical English action first for washing, eating matzah and maror, making the sandwich, dining, and beginning grace; the Portuguese remains an optional liturgical witness.
sequences.rationale: These source-order pages preserve the table’s full transition from the second cup through symbolic eating and the meal into communal grace.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Artur Carlos de Barros Basto and the digitization; identify the CC0 1.0 source.

sequences.title: Portuguese cumulative closing songs
sequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests that these are cumulative end-of-Seder songs, provide a simple refrain and an English synopsis, and make Portuguese participation optional.
sequences.rationale: Four consecutive end pages preserve the rising count of Echad Mi Yodea and the complete narrative chain of Chad Gadya.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Artur Carlos de Barros Basto and identify the CC0 1.0 historical source.

sequences.title: Cyclical history and an interfaith opening
sequences.beginnerContext: State that this is an opt-in Battlestar fan adaptation, explain the cyclical-history premise, and label the Gâyatrî Mantra as a Hindu text rather than Jewish liturgy.
sequences.rationale: The source’s first two records establish its recurring-history lens and complete the deliberately cross-cultural opening before the plate ritual begins.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit all four compilers and Open Siddur; separately verify franchise quotation and Gâyatrî Mantra attribution.

sequences.title: Fan-remapped plate and invitation
sequences.beginnerContext: Explain the ordinary parsley, salt water, matzah, and invitation first, then show how the fan text playfully remaps each element to fictional exile and survival.
sequences.rationale: These adjacent records keep the plate symbolism, ritual eating, invitation to those in need, and wish for peace together as one adapted ritual movement.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit Lieberman, Ogden, Bruch, Varady, and Open Siddur; preserve open-license provenance and review franchise references.

sequences.title: Second cup and fictional migration story
sequences.beginnerContext: Give the real Exodus story elsewhere in full; introduce this only as the fan Haggadah’s fictional second-cup story about exile, migration, danger, and searching for home.
sequences.rationale: Three consecutive records preserve the second-cup toast and the full Battlestar migration narrative without confusing it with the project’s foundational Exodus account.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit all compilers and Open Siddur; review quoted and paraphrased franchise story material separately.

sequences.title: Third cup and responsibility for creation
sequences.beginnerContext: Introduce this as a fictional ethical meditation: the characters face responsibility for creating life and for the consequences they tried to disown.
sequences.rationale: The paired records set up the third cup as guilt and complete the source’s argument about moral responsibility for what people create.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit all compilers and Open Siddur and review any direct television dialogue before runtime approval.

sequences.title: Fellowship and interfaith closing
sequences.beginnerContext: Name the fourth cup as fellowship, identify the closing Sanskrit prayer as an Upanishadic text, and explain that the final destination is fictional Earth.
sequences.rationale: The source’s final ritual records move from responsibility to the fleet into a prayer for truth, light, and life and then the fan adaptation’s closing refrain.
sequences.creditNotes: Credit the compilers and Open Siddur; verify the Upanishadic prayer translation and franchise material separately.
